title: Realization of the Ruby Lattice Antiferromagnet in Layered Transition-Metal Fluorides
authors: Harald O. Jeschke, Daniel Guterding, Pratyay Ghosh
abstract: The antiferromagnet on the ruby lattice is expected to host a range of exotic emergent phenomena, yet its material realization has remained elusive. Here we show that the layered transition metal fluorides CsBaFe$_3$F$_{12}$ and CsBaCr$_3$F$_{12}$ with Fe$^{3+}$ and Cr$^{3+}$ ions realize only slightly distorted ruby lattice geometries with spin moments $S=5/2$ and $S=3/2$, respectively. Their microscopic Hamiltonians, calculated with DFT energy mapping, are dominated by short-ranged antiferromagnetic interactions within the ruby layers. Classical Monte Carlo simulations reveal strong frustration in both compounds, with local Néel correlations on the hexagonal plaquettes and distinct long-range ordering tendencies governed by weaker triangular links. For CsBaFe$_3$F$_{12}$, the calculated thermodynamic behaviour is consistent with the experimentally reported magnetic ordering scale. For CsBaCr$_3$F$_{12}$, classical Monte Carlo and Luttinger-Tisza analysis reveal competing low-energy ordering wave vectors, strong finite-size sensitivity, and a tendency toward incommensurate order. Overall, our results establish these fluorides as experimentally accessible ruby-lattice antiferromagnets and provide quantitative predictions for future neutron-scattering studies.
summary: This paper identifies two layered transition-metal fluorides as experimental realizations of the elusive ruby lattice antiferromagnet. By combining DFT and Monte Carlo simulations, the authors show that these materials host incommensurate spin-spiral states driven by frustrated exchange interactions. The work provides quantitative predictions for future neutron-scattering experiments.
main_problem: The difficulty in finding experimental material realizations of the ruby lattice antiferromagnet, which is expected to host exotic emergent phenomena and topological orders.
main_result: The study identifies CsBaFe3F12 and CsBaCr3F12 as excellent candidates for 2D ruby-lattice magnetism, demonstrating that both compounds realize an incommensurate spiral low-temperature state.
method: The researchers used DFT+U energy mapping to extract Heisenberg exchange interactions, Classical Monte Carlo simulations to study thermodynamic behavior, and Luttinger-Tisza analysis to identify magnetic ordering vectors.
model_or_system: The physical systems are the layered transition-metal fluorides CsBaFe3F12 (S=5/2) and CsBaCr3F12 (S=3/2) featuring a distorted ruby lattice geometry. The model is a Heisenberg antiferromagnet on a 12-site basis lattice.
key_observables: Magnetic specific heat, spin structure factor S(q), magnetic ordering temperature (Neel temperature), and magnetic order parameter.
important_parameters: Exchange interaction strengths (J1 through J7), Hubbard U, spin magnitude S, and the stiffness (kappa) of the energy landscape.
main_assumptions: The study uses classical Monte Carlo rather than quantum Monte Carlo and assumes the magnetic interactions can be accurately captured by a Heisenberg Hamiltonian.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the ruby lattice geometry and ground state; Figure 2 displays crystal structures, exchange paths, and exchange interaction values as a function of U.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the ruby lattice problem, presents the structural and exchange characterization of the two fluorides via DFT, details the Monte Carlo and Luttinger-Tisza results for magnetic ordering, and concludes with an analytical framework explaining the difference in magnetic stiffness between the two compounds.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Ruby lattice and the ground state of the classical Heisenberg antiferromagnet. The ideal ruby lattice geometry constructed from a periodic tiling of trian- gles, squares, and hexagons. The unit cell is indicated by dashed lines. The lattice contains two symmetry-inequivalent nearest-neighbour bonds, one set forming triangular motifs and another forming hexagonal loops. The classical antiferro- magnetic Heisenberg model on this lattice has a ground state in which each hexagon develops local N´eel order, with a rela- tive 120◦spin canting between neighbouring hexagons.

FIG. 2. Crystal structure, exchange paths and exchange interactions. a, d Crystal structures of CsBaFe3F12 and CsBaCr3F12 viewed along the c direction. b, e The first seven exchange paths that make up the ruby lattice of CsBaFe3F12 and CsBaCr3F12. c, f Values of the seven nearest neighbour exchange interactions as a function of on-site interaction strength U for CsBaFe3F12 and CsBaCr3F12.

FIG. 3. Specific heat and magnetic ordering tempera- tures of CsBaFe3F12 as a function of simulated lattice size. a Specific heat of CsBaFe3F12 calculated for different system sizes. b N´eel temperatures extracted from maxima of specific heat in a. The dashed lines are guides to the eye.

FIG. 4. Ground state spin configuration, Equal-time spin structure factor and magnetic order parameter of CsBaFe3F12. a Real space spin configuration at T=0.01 K (proxy for the ground state). b Equal-time spin structure factor, S(q), at T=0.01 K. The data are shown in the Carte- sian qx-qy plane. The central hexagon represents the first Brillouin zone (BZ). The calculation used a 24 × 24 supercell and a 48 × 48 grid for the momentum q. c Magnetic order parameter calculated from the overlap of finite-temperature spin configurations with the magnetic ground state in a.

FIG. 5. Specific heat and magnetic ordering tempera- tures of CsBaCr3F12 as a function of simulated lattice size. a Specific heat of CsBaCr3F12 calculated for different system sizes. b N´eel temperatures read off from peak maxima in a. The dashed lines are a guide to the eye.
